does: This paper presents a novel framework for measuring semantic similarity between sparse autoencoder (SAE) features across different layers of language models using optimal transport theory. Rather than representing features as single decoder vectors, the authors represent each feature as an activation-weighted distribution over hidden states. They project these distributions into a shared reference space and compute Wasserstein distances to establish a unified semantic metric. The method is proven to be invariant to activation rescaling and stable under perturbations, enabling both cross-layer feature matching and circuit compression into interpretable supernodes. The distributional representation recovers true matches under finite-sample conditions and unifies two previously separate problems into a single mathematical framework.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature using sparse autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability of language models, extending prior work on feature attribution and circuit analysis. It responds to scalability challenges in existing feature-matching approaches that typically rely on simple vector comparisons or limited heuristics. The paper connects to optimal transport theory, which has increasingly been applied to machine learning for measuring similarity between distributions and comparing representations across different spaces. The research also builds on recent work emphasizing that features in neural networks are better understood through their activation patterns rather than isolated weights, and extends circuit analysis methods that have struggled with multi-layer feature alignment and meaningful compression of feature interactions.
enables: This work enables more scalable and principled approaches to mechanistic interpretability of large language models by providing a robust method for tracking features across model depth. It opens possibilities for automatically discovering and compressing meaningful feature circuits into human-interpretable supernodes, which could accelerate the pace of neural network reverse-engineering. The framework could extend to other architectures and modalities beyond language models that use sparse autoencoders or similar interpretability tools. The theoretical guarantees around stability and invariance provide foundations for building practical tools that practitioners can rely on. Future work could leverage this semantic matching to understand feature redundancy, information flow through networks, and potentially inform model compression or editing techniques based on identified feature relationships.
